speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four of the interview-- the interview with Yvette Lennon, on February 12, 1995. You were talking about the double bass.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The trouble is now we needed-- the conductor needed desperately a double bass. The-- she gave to the mandolins the-- the part of the double bass, but it wasn't what she wanted. So when I was watching the double bass-- the reason is I'm laughing, because my mother bought me a double bass in Greece. She says, I want you to learn the double bass. I say, I'm not going to touch this thing. I say, I was only so tall, you know. It's not a cello. A cello is, when you see it, it's big enough. But the double bass, is you stand up, and I was so little!

speaker: interviewee
text: So she says-- she says, no, you see a girl playing a double bass? That would be something new in Greece. I say, she was ahead of her time, my mother-- everything she wanted us to do this, to do that. So it stayed there. I said to her, well, if you want me to play the double bass, then I want to learn the drums too. So she-- first, she says, all right. Then I learned six months the drums. But I didn't have the chance then when the Nazis came. And they took the double bass and everything, you know. So I didn't have that chance. And I couldn't even care less about the double bass.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but what happened, in the-- the teacher wanted a double bass badly there in the orchestra-- leader. And there we had the double was-- was standing there. And I was looking at the double bass, and I said, it reminds me of my home. The double bass is in this corner. [LAUGHS] Nobody is playing it, you know, like I didn't. I had it there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- my sister, with her smartness, she says-- she says to my-- to the-- it's in the book. That story is in the book, you know, the It says my sister said, the reason is that they knew a accordion player in camp. As new transports were coming, and so they put her in the orchestra. Now, what would they need now three accordions? She could play marches like me, but she couldn't play like my sister. My sister played classical. Nobody could do that with the accordion. So she was fine. My sister was thinking, two accordions, what are they going to do now with the two of you for the marches? One of them would be thrown out. And you-- you're gonna die. So I say, you are going to learn the double bass. Me? I say-- I say--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --you-- you're going to play the double bass. Oh sure, I'll pick it up and I'll play it. I said-- she says, no, I will ask the conductor, Alma. She says-- I say, you're crazy! I say, here we're gonna have lessons? I said, you know where we are?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, well, I'll ask her. So she asked. And she thought it was a good idea. I said she's crazy too. I said two of you are crazy.

speaker: interviewee
text: She decided. And she asked Hessler, the one who was in charge of the music department-- another, you know, SS who was in charge there-- and he says we need-- we have a double bass here. And there is a girl who we want to learn-- we want her to teach-- to learn the double bass. He says, is it possible to get a double bass player from the men orchestra so to teach her? He says, well, I have to think about that. So we don't know what he did. But they wrote a book about the men's orchestra, and they wrote that they were speculating whether they gonna let somebody learn the double bass. But I didn't know then until I got that book that-- here, now. So they said-- so he decided. They decided we'd like a man from the men's orchestra come and teach me three times a week. So they-- he got the permission. And so this man came. He-- he was a very-- he wasn't a double bass player, also. He played the cello and other things. So he was very good musician. And he start teaching me an hour a week. He had the permission.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put up front-- you know, when you come-- men coming. They're not supposed to come in-- that this person is going to come and teach a girl some music-- so he could get in. Otherwise, they wouldn't let him in. And he start teaching me. And I found out that it wasn't so difficult. And well, he taught me for about two months.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the-- this SS comes back-- Hessler. And he said, where is the girl who plays the double bass? Isn't she playing with you? He says, what-- how do you know to play music in one minute, and in months. She's just playing almost two months, that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but I learned something. When he wasn't there I was in the other room practicing my double bass. They gave me the piece-- piece of music that they were playing with the orchestra. And I started studying by myself for the bass. And I learned it. And I was following them when they were playing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one of the girls say, she can play it. She says, you can play? I say, what can you play? I say, 'La Danza'. So she says, all right. So she played, and she was so happy, the teacher, that I learned, that already we will be start playing with the orchestra.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was so excited. He said to her, when I-- when we-- we played-- and then she said to the-- the teacher said, you know, her mother wanted her to play the double bass when she was in Greece. But she didn't have the chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: See now they are human. They suddenly get human. They say, where's your mother now? He asked me. The teacher says, she went through the gas chamber. He says, [GERMAN]-- in German, means shame. Yeah, now he's sorry, because he sees we are human, you know? He says, how old are you? I say, I'm 14. He says, one day-- he says, when you get out of here, you may earn your living with that. And we all look at each other. Are we ever going to get out of here? You know, and that, which is the truth. This happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got the job-- this is how-- what I got out of here. I use this. But the thing is, how they become so human all of a sudden when they see you are a person, that you can talk, and you can-- you are normal. That-- well, anyway, we-- so I became-- now nobody-- I couldn't lose the job. Because nobody played the double bass-- just me-- the accordion into the orchestra. But I still played marches alternating with the other one. One day I'll play, one day she would play, because it was very cold outside. Playing-- your fingers were like ice. You-- we are playing with a gloves and everything. When you come in, and, you know, and you warm your hands, it's like the pain is so bad if you go next to the heat, you know. But we had to play outdoors like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and then we gave concerts. We gave concerts in the middle of the two camps. So the girls on Sunday listened to the concerts. And also, the Nazis always say, we're coming over. You're gonna play something. You know, they-- and they brought Mengele. They brought many of these big officers that they had there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one woman was there, Irma Grese. She was very beautiful. She was very young, 22 years old. And she was one of the SS women. But the book on her, she-- she-- she turned out to be-- she was a very bad person. We didn't even know. She was so nice to the orchestra. She knew us by names. And she-- when we walked, she would just wave to us like we know them, you know. And that, I tell you, what happened in Bergen-Belsen with her. But that's why I'm mentioning this woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Kramer was our commandant. Would you believe? Kramer-- Kramer used to come often. He was the commandant of Auschwitz. And in the orchestra-- like they wanted to hear music, you know. And then they'll go and stand next to-- to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Say is-- a girl is playing with the girl next-- next to that-- to that girl, next to me. Sometimes he'd stand-- he'll stand next to me, you know, watching play. And-- and you think you know-- you think, oh, I almost think he's a little devil somewhere in that-- with an outfit, you know, like when I saw him standing there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was nice. And he would say, who needs something? Because I'm going to Berlin for the instrument. And we raised our hand. I need grease for my bells, and this. Can you imagine? We tell that to the most-- the highest commandant there that he's going to buy us something for us. It didn't make sense. It's-- it's so crazy. So anyway, we-- we did our work there. And-- and then the time was passing as I tell-- Did-- did he actually buy something for you or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes! He had. We needed things for the instruments. Where will we get in Berlin? There was nothing there, otherwise-- if the string broke, they-- they would have, you know, some-- a new strings. He would bring them. He liked music. They all loved music.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the meantime, I had-- now my-- my brother was in the men's camp. And he also was in the orchestra. And he also-- he knew that-- I mean, he knew we were in the orchestra. And he knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, another woman, who was also one of the big ones that came to hear the orchestra, her name was Mandel. I don't know what happened to her. And she came that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, when my teacher came to teach me the lesson, she brought my brother with me. He gave him an instrument to pretend that they thought that-- he won't say he wanted to see us, you know? He brought-- he says, I'll bring you-- as he like see-- like this. You can see your sister. And he brought him. But-- and then I went in the kitchen talking to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this Mandel walks in. And she sees my brother. And my brother, as soon he saw her, he ran out. And-- and she start running after him. She went to follow him. And then she stopped him. And then they both came back. And I was shaking, because there's an-- an incident that happened before that was very bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said, why are you running? She says to him, who are you? He says, well, I came with the-- with the-- with the one who's teaching my sister. She works in the orchestra. She says, well why are you running? She said, get back there. And he came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it wouldn't work out like that for the other-- the two mandolins, the two girls that I told you that were the mandolin players. They had a brother in the orchestra. And they, one day, the brother sent them a note with a worker, a worker who's coming out of camp, to see how they are, that he's all right. He got the note. So when he go through the door, they searched him. They found the note. And then, immediately--

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I'm OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then immediately, they-- they-- they send the two girls up there. They say they came up and caught the two sisters. They give them 12 lashes each. And they didn't get the note. They didn't write the note. And they came-- they came back so sick. And then they beat up the brother, and he died-- just for that note. That's why my brother got so scared. And I was terrified when he saw him inside the block, you know. And-- well, anyway, that's what happened to them. And then the girl-- the sister, the younger sister, had typhus, and she died. In the orchestra, she had typhus, and she was the only one left from her whole family. She had brothers, and her sister-in-law had a baby-- was expecting. They all went to the gas chambers, because she was expecting. The brother, he died working there. And her whole family was wiped out. And she stayed alive. Her name is Julie. I'll have to tell you after something about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, and then, came that-- now we're started to have typhus. Things started to get closer to the end. And I saw my brother. And my brother was walking in-- in between the two camps, because they had work there to go to this lager.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where they went, I don't know what they did. That was near the gas chambers. They used to bring stuff, like clothing, whatever, I don't know. And that's when my girlfriend, Olga, was there-- you know, I told you-- working there. But what she was doing, I'll never know. Because she would never talk about anything about the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, my brother, as he was passing, we saw him. And they called that your brother is passing. So we start waving. And with that other one, the-- the one who was 45 years old, who was a violinist-- so he said-- I tell-- I-- I-- I shouted to him. I say, are we going to get out of here? So the violinist was a pessimist, actually. Well, I don't blame him. He said, yes, through the chimney. I said, oh, you, I said.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then my sister too, always talking about the chimney. I said to my sister-- she was-- she was down. My sister was a pessimist, and I was an optimist. She would say, one, we-- we going to die here with the gas chambers are right across the street from us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, no we're not! I say, what makes you think we are not? Where do you think we are going-- we are going to get sicker, and sicker. You think they're not going to put us in the gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, but we are not going to the gas chambers. So she says, you are a nut. I say, and you-- I say, I-- I know we're not going to go. If you want to go, you are sure you are going to the gas chambers, why don't you go now-- like this. We're suffering here anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she says, leave her alone, the other girl tells her. And I say, I'm going to pray for you too. I say, that you're not going to go either. You know, like I say, or believe in God. And I did. I did pray hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- I said, dear God. I say, this time you always answer my prayers, but this time I'm asking you a very big favor. I don't want to die here. I say, I don't want my sister to die here. I don't want anyone-- but I said, please. Don't let us die. I'm too young to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: And would you believe it? That that made me-- I went through so much after that. The-- the thought that I'm not going to die there made me survive all of these things that I went through-- take the typhus was number one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I couldn't eat. The food that we have it, I couldn't eat it. I just was getting very-- I-- I had no appetite whatsoever. So I had to run in the bathroom. They gave us a private bathroom for the-- for the orchestra for five people-- just us. But the others were in the street, you know, all open up. Because it was in my little bathroom-- and they were there. So we-- and there was heat in there and everything. And then I used to hide there every time it was lunchtime. Here people are dying from food they want to eat, and I have to disappear. And my sister said, if you don't eat, I'll kill you. And then is when my brother came again.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, why aren't you eating? I say, I'm not hungry! So-- and I stay-- I said-- and then I started losing. And I said, I didn't have the strength to go to the other concentration camp that we were going to go, because I was losing my strength. So like this, I hardly ate up to when they took us away from there. So anyway, now things were coming to a close. And we saw, one day, this happened. The-- as we were in-- we were in the block. And suddenly, we hear-- we saw the German Nazis running here and there, and here and there, screaming. And we didn't know what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we found out that-- now this is the-- the sonnerkommander, they called it. You know, the gas chambers, they have 40 people working for this. When people would come, they would-- they waited what they would do, put the people in the gas chambers and all that. Then how they get out, whatever, I didn't want to know, really, but I know now. And what they would do-- and they needed 40 people to do the job for them. The Nazis weren't gonna do it. So this time they chose 40 Greek, young Jewish men to-- for-- and they-- what they did with them, they worked there for three months. And then they threw them in the gas chambers, because they didn't know-- they didn't want the world to know what they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that day, the Greeks, they were so annoyed. They took two Nazis and threw the two Nazis in the gas chamber-- the ones who were telling them to do this. And then the chaos, the noise, the screaming everywhere, everywhere-- what are they doing? And then they said, the-- some-- then we found out that somebody killed two Nazis, and now they are all punished, or whatever they were going to do to them. I don't know what happened after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then all I know-- that a couple a weeks later, a week later, the men were leaving. They were going-- many men from the-- from the-- from the men's camp, they had them on lines. They won't know where they were taking them. And that's one of them was my brother. So we never knew where he went. They told us that if they-- if they put you in trains, don't get in, because they are bringing you to another camp where there is gas chambers so that you don't get upset. I say, even if we went-- I say, they can-- they can stop us. They can put us in a gas chamber even if we don't want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the thing is, they took them in another-- took them to Germany. I think they took them away, because of this that had happened. And also, they ruined two gas chambers. They blew up the gas chambers. The-- the Greeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Greeks. There were five of them. And, of course, they-- they punished them at the end. I think I have something on them. I say, the one who did it-- I don't know if he's alive, if he made it. But they-- after-- they-- they now, the Nazis-- oh, not the Nazis, the British are coming closer. They knew, now, it's coming to an end. Because the men knew from other people. Sometimes they used to work outdoors, and a political person knew passed, and they would tell them that we're coming to an end. It's coming to an end. It's gonna end soon. Because as the British was coming closer and closer-- oh, we're hoping that we make it. And-- and suddenly, we see all these men going. And now we don't know anymore what happened to my brother. And-- and then we continue now, something happened very bad to us, the orchestra, in the meantime. Now, I told you, the-- the block leader was Alma Rose, the-- that famous person. And she-- now there were also, as I say, there were also German prisoners who were real Germans. For political reasons they were there. There was one, Frau Smith, one German woman who made friends with Alma Rose. Now she met her, they both speak German. So they decided that she had a nice little bungalow for her own-- whatever she was, whatever she lived. She lived in the camp. Maybe she had a better room than everybody else. And she brought-- she invited Alma Rose always to have, every Sunday, to have dinner at her house together. She had more food, and that, and more privilege. So Alma always used to go there to pass the evening. And one Sunday, she comes back at 11 o'clock. We were all in bed. She was in the other room, but in her private little room. Suddenly, she's screaming and screaming.

speaker: interviewee
text: We all wake up, you know. What was wrong with her? The block leader immediately got up. And she ran to the hospital with her, and brought her to the hospital. And the next thing, she comes back-- she died. Now, we don't know whether she killed her. Maybe she would-- because they had-- there's two reasons now-- from food poisoning-- why come she didn't die? That's another story that came out there. She didn't die. Alma Rose died. And she had the same food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and the other thing is not only that, that Alma was very-- I mean, OK. They were very good friends and everything. But they found out Alma, because she was famous, they decided that they are going to let her go and play for soldiers to get her out of this mess-- of this mess where she is. She told her. And she was thinking why her and not me. Maybe that's why she-- she poisoned her. We'll never know. But this one girl that I know, [INAUDIBLE], she worked for the-- at the hospital with-- and her-- one who became her husband. They send the autopsy in Auschwitz, and they say that she died from-- not from food poisoning. She didn't die from food poisoning. But the thing is-- no from food poisoning, that she-- she didn't do it to herself or whatever-- something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They will lie there too if they want to. You know, then the next day, oh, we were all so upset. Suddenly, we found ourselves with no teacher. What are we going to do without a conductor? We were-- are they coming here? No. No. I think so. Somebody's coming They'll-- they'll see the note on the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, maybe it's my phone. What time is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's 10 of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's them. Somewhere here-- and they're calling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still taping?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: The mother's here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you want to stop taping for a moment?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Let's pause the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Alma died. And we played another two weeks by ourselves. There was a woman who was the pianist, and she directed us a bit. And then while we were playing-- and a-- a messenger comes and tells us that we have to-- we-- the orchestra-- get up. And the Jews, separate yourselves. Because we had the others who were not Jewish. And they say immediately to go up, the doctor wants to see us-- up the next camp. And we went there. And they didn't do anything. And then they returned us. And then we stuck in between the two camps. They made us stop right there and then. They say, you're getting into the trains. And that's because it was taking to Bergen-Belsen, only the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we-- we go there, we were very scared, because we thought that because they told us, they don't think that in Germany they are-- no. No, they told us, the men from the camp told us, you better-- when they put you in the trains, don't get into the trains, because they are going to send you in another gas chamber. Because you know there isn't one here. So-- so we had this in our minds. So how can you not get in in the trains? They'll shoot you, they feel. So We got in the trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we had five day journey. And I saw Berlin as we were passing, looking from the window. And we're saying, there's people walking. We thought that we're not people anymore. And we're like seeing people down with golden suitcases. So anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: How fast were you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How fast? Was-- was the train going very quickly or slowly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal, you know, but it took five days to go to-- from Austria-- from where we were in Bergen-Belsen to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could the people--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Hanover.

speaker: interviewer
text: --could the people that you passed see in the train and see you in there-- or no, there was no contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if they saw us. We saw them. They were getting out of the stations, you know. And we saw them walking and looking so smart and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't know if they knew what that train meant.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if they knew. But they-- they put us in Bergen-Belsen. Bergen-Belsen was one of the most horrible camps there was. There was six months more to go before the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we get into Bergen-Belsen. First, we-- when we walked in, we-- the man who-- who told us to walk, he said-- we got in. He says, don't think that there's no gas chambers here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, there wasn't. But he-- but he said that there was a tiny one. There was one, but that was for the dead people. But they wouldn't do this in Germany-- kill people, so that they see it-- so-- in front of them. Because there was houses nearby. It was a beautiful place, Belsen. They put a lot of concentration camps there. So when we went there, the-- this man took us there and-- into the camp. And then we saw all these people. The-- the camp wasn't finished. They were just building it. There was very bad barracks. And then there were tents, because there was not enough room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the men said, as we were walking. No. He says-- OK. First, he put us in the tent. And it was February. It was freezing. The tent blew open, you know, from the wind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he came and picked us up. And he had told us, don't-- don't think that there's no gas chamber here. There is one, he says.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- so he sent us to a warehouse. We were all sleeping on the floor. And when he came in the-- in the morning, he says come on. He says 100 of you-- out. So we thought about the 100 that fit in the gas chamber. I say, now they have no room, they're going to put us there? That was the most-- the frightening thing I had since Auschwitz and then all that-- much more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when we-- we were marching, and there was, you know, like side roads. And we never passed. We just keep on going-- and then another one, and another one. When we came to the very last one, we saw there's no more walk. There's no more sidewalk to pass. Then I was holding [INAUDIBLE] this girl's friend, Julie, who was with us. And we were holding-- we knew we were going to the gas chambers. We were-- the feeling, you cannot explain it. We were shaking. And I was saying my prayers. I said, please, don't let us suffer so much. I hope we die fast, we don't-- and all that. We really are scared to death. That's the first-- the only place where I really was very scared. So-- but once we walked a little bit more, there was a tiny little area that we didn't see, a small little street. We couldn't see it as we passed. He says, turn left. And we went left. And then they put us with-- at the barracks-- you know, the-- in a-- in a barrack. The orchestra was separated-- some here, some there. And they put us to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- we had to be-- you know, to put things for the planes that they do-- made of cellophane, whatever they use it for. We had to do some kind of a, you know, like this, making things for the plane, like making up like a plait-- a plait-- whatever they say when they wore-- you wear in the hair-- when they make plaits. So anyway, see, we were doing it. But we were trying to throw it-- the most of it in the oven, you know, when we were working there. And then we saw somebody coming. We slide needle in the door. We say-- we start working. So there, my sister was coming to work one day, and she fell. She fell and she broke her leg. And she was behind me, I didn't even know she was. [INAUDIBLE] I was-- when they told me you're sister is in the hospital. I say, what's the matter? So I went running there. She says the ice. She-- she-- you know, she-- she slipped in the ice. So, and then they put her in a hospital across from us. And they were not taking good care of her. And she was crying. She was telling me-- I could hear her through the wires. She was saying, she says, please get me out of here. Please. I'm gonna die here. So I asked one of the singers who was a German Jew. And I say, please, can you ask Kramer? Kramer knew us. You see, they became the commandants, and Irma Grese, in that camp-- there. She-- like there were in Auschwitz, they became there. So-- They went to Belsen, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Belsen. I didn't know that. She said, could you tell Kramer, maybe since we were in the orchestra, maybe he does something. She did. And they took her back. They took her back to-- to the block. But she couldn't go outside and stand out in the court to-- to be counted. So then the block leader sent her back to the other camp. And then again, back-- they brought her back. At the end, she-- OK. Well, my sister, in meantime, she started to feel weak. Her heart was very weak. She-- she stayed with us. She ordered-- they ordered that they count her from inside. And the food was nothing-- so little. Because we're all having-- now everybody was having dysentery-- everybody. If you see the Bergen-Belsen tape-- I have it-- it was a chaos-- skeletons all over the place. They were all dying. And they were throwing them on the pits. They put the prisoners to throwing them in a big pit. And the-- those days, what they got is from not eating. They didn't give us-- they didn't have anymore. They gave us food-- one soup a day. No-- no-- no-- how do you call-- bread or nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were all getting so thin. And some of them were having swollen necks-- no swollen legs, and then the neck. If-- if the swelling comes in the neck, it means you're going to die within three days. So every day when we got up, we were looking at our necks to see if it's swollen. No-- we were happy. It doesn't matter if we were skeleton, as long as we weren't swollen. So-- I'm rushing it up, you know, so because of that. So anyway, when-- in the meantime, my sister, a woman, who used to be a doctor, says, your sister will have only one week to live. She will die. She needs more food. And I said, what shall I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said-- my sister told me, listen. Say you remember Irma Grese, she says. Now she says, why don't you go and ask her for-- to give you a job. Maybe you get a bit more food from anywhere, the kitchen, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what-- what does she care about me now? She says, you go. Go. tell her that I'm sick. And I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went all the way to the office-- which I'm not allowed to go as far as the office-- but I went. And she saw me. She came up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was wearing a scarf. And I-- I was very-- I don't know how thin. [? They didn't even ?] have mirrors, but I was like the skeletons. And she-- she gave me a slap in the face. And I went running back. And I said to her, see, she knows me. She says she didn't see you well. She says because you were wearing a scarf. I say you have to go again. I say, come quickly what is she gonna do to me? So she says-- and then I sat outside, because we have no more work. Because the British were coming closer and closer. So they all knew that. So the work-- they stopped the work. So I was sitting outdoors there in the-- in the-- there was a little grass. And I saw her passing-- Irma Grese. She-- I saw her walking in the main road. And I say, now I'm gonna face her face-to-face. Maybe this time she will see me. So I ran then, and she stopped, like who are you, you know, like she could kill me. She says, oh, wait a minute, you are the girl from the orchestra. She says, I say, where is the orchestra now? I say well, we don't have. I say it's 1/2-- they're all different blocks. There's some in my block. She says, what do you want? I say, you know my sister is very ill. You remember my sister. She says the fat one? My sister used to be a little heavy. She wasn't-- considering to all of us, she was fatter. She said-- the fat-- yeah. She's very sick. I explained to her what happened. She says, really? She says, come with me. She says where is the-- where are-- are there some girls from the orchestra in the block? I say, yes. OK, I want to go in.

speaker: interviewee
text: She comes in with me in the block. And everybody stood up. They all got so scared, you know, when a Nazi comes in. And she comes--
